data.text:
INT: Tape 21 of Mark Verstandig. We are talking now about the period, end of '44, beginning 1945, where you were in the army, and the places that you were stationed in. Were the Germans completely-- were these areas free of Germans by now? 

MV: When? 

INT: Were these areas in Poland free of Germans? 

MV: Yeah. The areas were free of Germans. We went to Bydgoszcz. And the Germans were outside Bydgoszcz. But we were in Bydgoszcz already, and Bydgoszcz was free. And I threw out this mayor, who was a well-known antisemite and an ND, belongs to the-- to this national party, to the antisemitic party, and then installed a socialist-- a social Democrat one, PPS. 

And next day-- ah, that was the same day, I think. The same night at dinner, there were-- that-- we had dinner in a hall. In the school hall was a restaurant-- was a canteen for-- for the officers of-- for officers of the army, of the Polish army. And I was sitting there by myself, and I saw next to me-- I saw it was a table. And Major [POLISH], he was a political commissar. He was a major. He was later Minister for Interior in Poland and of-- and-- before he defected. 

He was a well-known communist. And I could see that he is sitting there with some youngsters, and the discussion was with them to organize the-- a kind of a Komsomol, a kind of a communist-- of-- of-- of a communist youth organization. And the youngsters told him that before the war they were scouts. And they were connected with the church, and so on and so on and so on. 

In the end, I don't think that they went-- I could hear because-- what they were talking about, it was next table. And they went away and this Mr.-- the Major [POLISH] took them to-- showed them to the door and came back with a bottle of vodka, with two glasses, and sat next to me. And told me-- and now he sat next to me. 

I told him, look, what are you talking about with those youngsters? You could see they are NDs. They were connected-- they are scouts connected with the church, and you wanted to make out of them-- to make out the communist, young communists, a Komsomol. You are wasting your time. 

So he gave me a-- he gave me a political lesson. Look, we will be communists. We'll come to an agreement, and to come close, rather with the NDs than with the Szalasia. The Pilsudski Szalasia were close to the Germans, but [POLISH] was always pro-Russian. I told him, what are you talking nonsense? [POLISH] was pro-Russian when there was a-- a reactionary Russian regime of the Czar. But not now. Now they are communists in Russia. 

I said, look, in political language, it doesn't matter. If one is poor Russian, the change of regime doesn't matter. You-- you will see that we will make an agreement. We'll come to close rather with the NDs than with the Pilsudski people. All right. 

And by the way, he told me. I wanted to tell you something. You threw out the major-- the-- the mayor, the ND mayor, and installed a PPS man, a Social Democrat one as mayor. May I tell you? I threw out your mayor, your socialist mayor, and reinstalled the ND. 

And I said, why did you do it? He said, look, I told you why before. And if you want to know, I will tell you something. He says your duty here to see that the bakeries should bake bread. He says your duty here to see that the schools reopen. And all other things, which are very important, that the people should accept in the meantime, the Polish money, the old money. That is your duty. And don't mix in politics. Don't-- don't do anything. 

For politics, that's me. And your duty-- your duty is to see that bread should be baked and people should accept the money, and schools. I got the lesson. I got the lesson, and-- and that was that. 

By the way, I remember the name I mentioned to you-- I mentioned a name before of this man in-- in the Justice Department, Dr. Sommerstein tried to-- through him to get me a job. That was Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], a famous-- a famous lawyer. He was later the-- the representative of Poland in the Nuremberg-- Nuremberg trials. His name, real name, was [NON-ENGLISH]. And-- and he was Dr. [NON-ENGLISH]. 

And the name of the doctor, which I wanted to come to help my wife, a friend of mine in gymnasium, was [NON-ENGLISH]. As a matter of fact, I read later that he himself was going around helping Jewish people in-- in-- in hiding. But they sent me a-- a stranger to my wife, and the stranger was such a one that I would have never believed that he will stretch out his hand for help, and-- though that's a mixture of people. 

INT: At that time, the beginning of 1945-- 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: --in your job of looking after the administration of the various places, what sort of picture of life did you see? 

MV: [SIGHS] The same was everywhere. As far as the Jews are concerned, there were not-- there were not many Jews over there. But there were Jews in Warsaw. So I traveled through Warsaw when I went back from Bydgoszcz. 

So the situation was such that there were many people who helped Jews. 

Jews paid them. They were paid. There were many of them. There was a minority-- a minority, which was spying around-- spying around and-- and delivering Jews to the gestapo. I saw it myself while walking the streets of Warsaw. I mentioned, I think, maybe in the-- my-- before in one of the tapes. I mentioned it, that I saw this [NON-ENGLISH], where there were professional-- there are swarms of them, professional people whose-- whose business, whose industry, was to just smell out, to recognize a Jew, and take from the Jew whatever he possesses. 

If he has a ring, if he has gold, if he has dollars, and-- they were-- they-- they were taking away. If you-- if-- and they were letting you go for the time being. But they will know already where you are because they will send other people to walk behind you to see where-- where-- to see where you go, and to see where they can catch you tomorrow after tomorrow. When you didn't have anything, they delivered you to-- they-- they delivered you to the gestapo. Now, I could see it myself, and I mentioned it, maybe, before I could see it myself, on the street of Warsaw. 

This [NON-ENGLISH] grabbing a Jewish woman, and said, you are Jewish. And she didn't have enough money to pay. And they dragged her to the middle of the road and hailed a German car, and said, Jude. Take a Jude. The only-- that's the only word German they could-- they could speak, Jude. And I could see how people react because I was amongst the people walking the street of Warsaw. Other people there, some were indifferent. 

And others were just smiling, were just happy that that's going. But a lot of them were quite indifferent, or maybe they covered their indifference. Maybe they were protesting. They-- they would-- but I am sure if such a [NON-ENGLISH] something would have grabbed a Pole, trying to hand them over to the Germans, they would have reacted on the street against this man. But as far as the Jew is concerned, why should we bother? He didn't do it. The others did. It's quite-- it was quite OK. 

Matter of fact, in the village where I was hiding, you could see the-- really the picture of the society, of the Polish society as far-- as far as their approach to the Jewish question is concerned. You could see Markowski, you could see Valas, you could see the [POLISH] who-- where Mrs. Schreiber was hiding, and 10 other Jews. 

There was a fellow called [NON-ENGLISH], and-- who died maybe 95 years old, who died a couple of years ago. He wrote a little book about his memories, memoirs of-- of that time. And I quoted from his book about-- about Christian-- about Jewish children, homeless Jewish children. Their parents were killed, and they were going around sleeping in the open, or sleeping in a-- in-- in a barn or in a shed during the night. 

And he said in the morning or during the day, they should come to the door, kneel before the door, and say the father-- and-- and say the prayer-- you call this the prayer-- where they thank God for the daily bread. And he said who wouldn't give bread to such a child? But the fact is that not a single child in the [POLISH] area survived. They all were chased by Poles. 

It doesn't mean that the majority of Poles chased them. There were a minority of them, a minority who hated the Jews because they were agitated against the Jews. They were incited against the Jews. Or they were jealous that they didn't have an opportunity to hide the Jew and to get money. And they suspected that whoever hides a Jew is going to make a fortune. 

And therefore they chase them and they killed them. And another thing. Mrs. Schreiber, Helen, she looked like 100% Polish girl with-- 100% Pole. See, she-- she tells us about-- a-- a-- about a-- a dinner. I don't-- was-- I don't remember. It was-- it was a Christmas dinner or it was a dinner, Easter time. 

There were these local intelligentsia there, these-- all the people who had matriculation. Or even before matriculation, they all belonged to the intellectuals, to the-- amongst them was a priest. And she was sitting with them. She was sitting with them at the table during the dinner. And the priest said that after the war we will have to erect a monument for Hitler that he got rid of the Jews. And she noted in her-- she noted in her-- in an essay she wrote, she noted that [POLISH], the host, was embarrassed because she could hear what they are saying. 

It was not that he was-- he was embarrassed that Helen should listen to such a conversation, that he was-- she was witnessing a conversation where the priest will say all that. But this village reflected the situation in Poland. There were the [POLISH], who-- who-- who were hiding Jews, the Markowskis, the [POLISH], and the-- there-- there were on the hand-- and the Markowskis, and many others. 

And there were others, who behaved like Duskievici, who tried to find the Jews who placed, who deposited with them any goods there, or-- or-- or a Persian lamb coat and so on and so on. And they were-- they-- especially this minority were-- the minority was inciting the other Poles to do the job. And in the city itself, in the small city, the people were very glad to get rid of the Jews because everybody else has a-- had his mind already. 

He will take this shop. He will take this shop. He will take this house. In the largest city, like in Warsaw-- don't forget, in Warsaw itself I think about 20,000 Jews were saved in Warsaw, hiding. Because that was an urban town which-- I mean, the population was not-- it was not-- there were not shopkeepers looking for-- for-- for the Jewish shop in the neighborhood. They got some money. They-- they were hiding the Jews. 

But there were even people who after the Jews didn't have any money, they still kept them. And I know of a family-- I heard of a-- I noted of-- of some-- I was witnessing the people saying that they were hidden-- they-- they were hidden by a family, and after the money-- they didn't have any money, they didn't throw them out. And when the town was liberated, they gave them a few hundred zlotys that they shouldn't greet celebration without a penny. 

There were such people too. There were people, good people, bad people, a mix of everything. The only thing is-- the only thing between-- the only difference between Poland and any other neighboring state was that in Poland, the inciting against the Jews came from above. 

Came from the government in exile through the Delagatura. That was the real problem of the Jewish-- of the-- and that was going on-- and that was going on after-- after the liberation. After the liberation, people were killed daily. Daily, they were-- they were-- they were dragged out of buses, they were dragged out of trains, and they were killed. 

Matter of fact, see, I didn't-- don't remember that I told you that [NON-ENGLISH], a journalist in Stockholm, a Yiddish journalist in Stockholm, a-- an award-winning journalist. He wrote-- he published a book in Poland, in Polish, in Poland. And in that book, he describes how he came back to his-- to-- to his village. 

[POLISH], I think, was the village, not far south away from Bialystock or something like that. Anyhow, he came back to his village because he was already now a tank officer. He was with the division of tanks. And he was-- he got permission to go, to go to visit his-- his-- his village, although he was advised to be very careful. He was advised not to go. 

Well, he tells you that he went there to-- he had a talk with a-- with a police station, with a-- with the head of the police station in [POLISH]. And he told him that recently about several people-- [SPEAKING POLISH]. How-- how-- how would you [SPEAKING POLISH]? 

INT: A number of people. 

MV: Huh? A number. A number of people. A number of Jews. A number of Jews were killed, and they were buried here and there under a tree. When he asked him why not in the Jewish cemetery, he said, don't you know there is no more Jewish cemetery? 

The Jewish cemetery was taken over by the-- by-- by the peasants. Everybody took a little part of it, and there's no more Jewish cemetery. Where was the priest who told them-- who-- where was the priest who should have warned the people that you should not touch a cemetery? There was none. 

INT: How did you spend the-- your time between the beginning of '45 and liberation in May? 

MV: Beginning for '45? 

INT: And the liberation in May. 

MV: And the liberation? 

INT: In May, '45. 

MV: No. The liberation-- our liberation-- I was liberated in the-- on the 4th of August, 1944. 

INT: The end of the war. 

MV: And I was-- yeah, end of the-- that was, for me, the end of the war. I joined the government as a-- because I needed the guard-- I needed a guard at my-- at-- at the-- at the-- by the way, yeah. That was-- that. I needed the guard, and I was-- I had this [INAUDIBLE]. 

Now, this situation went on in Lublin until December, 1944. Later, I went to Bydgoszcz with the army. I went back, and the government-- the seat of the government moved at that time from Lublin to Warsaw. And I got a flat in Warsaw. And the seat of the government was in Warsaw. I was working in Warsaw. Again, only-- doing only legal work. 

INT: What-- what month was that in? 

MV: Beg your pardon? 

INT: What month was that in? 

MV: That was-- that must have been from January-- from January until-- until maybe September 1946-- 1945. The liberation then, the end of the war, was in May. But I wasn't there. For me, the end of the war didn't mean anything. It's just like-- like a-- I would read news about Zaire or about Congo. 

INT: What impression did Warsaw make on you when you got there in January? 

MV: When I got in January, I could see Jews coming out. Jews coming out of these-- of-- from hiding places. And they all were scared. And they all were happy that now is the time that they will be able to start a new life. Some of them thought of-- of emigrating to-- to-- to the United States, to England, to wherever-- wherever they can, and to Palestine. 

Nobody was really happy with the situation which existed. They didn't believe that they will be able to start life again in Poland. Everybody considered it, at that time, that for the time being, they are safe until situation-- until after the war, and then the situation will change, will quiet, the quieten down. And they will-- they will try to emigrate. But a number of people thought that they will be able to start again. 

You know, people go-- people try to-- to-- to go on where there is little resistance. He had a flat. He had a house. So he goes back, and he will start a life. But the trouble was with people coming back in smaller towns, and coming back to their-- to-- to-- to their houses, and the killing was going on every day. News came along every day that, in that town, in the other town, the Jews were-- came along, and they tried to-- to re-occupy their houses-- their houses, and they were killed. 

Oh, the-- the-- I know-- I know of a family, they-- they-- they-- they came back-- they came back and found a few members of the family killed. And the whole Jewish community, everybody who was still alive, ran. And they ran to the bigger cities. But in the bigger cities it was the same. 

In Cracow you-- was a problem in Cracow. There was killing in Cracow. There was killing in Warsaw, everywhere. But the people-- the press didn't write about it. The whole trouble was that they are going-- they were going around in train-- in trains and in buses, and picking out Jews and killing them. It's the only place in the world where people were killed after the war. 

INT: What did you do for the remainder of 1945? 

MV: The remainder of 1945, now, I worked for the government. And later, I decided-- ah, I decided that the time came to-- a time came to liquidate-- a time to take charge against-- against the killers. 

That is an interesting story, because when I was told by Valas that those people were murderers, that they established themself as the militia of the new government in that village, Tuchow, [POLISH] Tuchow. 

At that time, there was no Polish establishment, other establishment, in this town of Mielec. But Mielec was four kilometers-- four kilometers behind the train-- behind the-- the-- the-- oh, the war zone, where-- where-- behind the German positions, four kilometers. 

So I went to the Russian NAKED. Because the Russian NKVD was there, like the CIA, when they are together with the army. I went to their office and I told them that they should know that here is a militia, and they are murderers. They killed four people. They killed a-- a small boy. They wounded my wife, and so on. Told them the whole story. And they have got guns. They are other militia. 

And I was sure that within an hour they will be arrested, because there was-- there was just where-- where the front was. And I stayed for a night. I stayed toward the end. I could see nothing happened. So that was when I ran to Lublin. When I ran to Lublin to see Dr. Sommerstein. 

By the way, the men I talked to, this was Dr. [POLISH]. Dr. [POLISH] was over there. Anyhow, that was then, when I went to-- when I went to Lublin. And as far as the-- as far as these 11 people, the militia is concerned, nothing has been done. 

Dr. [POLISH] told me to see a certain officer in the-- in-- in-- in the-- in-- in the-- with security. 

INT: [NON-ENGLISH]. 

MV: Huh? 

INT: In the [NON-ENGLISH], was it? Or the Russian security? 

MV: Yeah, [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH], that he is in charge of all these investigations and tell him the story. I went over there. I told him the story-- very nice man. 

And he tells me, look, that's a militia. You should go to [POLISH]. The militia headquarters are in [POLISH]. And there is a Captain Vishnevski, a very nice man. Go to Vishnevski, tell him the story. Vishnevski will fix it. 

I went to Vishnevski. And Vishnevski heard the whole story and said, look, we'll work out a plan. And the plan was that we will go to Mielec and organize a-- how do you call, zlot. It means we call in all militia men from the whole district. 

INT: Roll call. 

MV: Huh? 

INT: Roll call or parade? 

MV: No, [INAUDIBLE] like we-- we will call-- we will-- we will send out-- and-- and we will send out to the local districts-- call to the local district's head that he should inform the-- the militia men, one-- one post after the other, that they should come and introduce themselves, that Captain Vishnevski want to shake hands and to see every one of them. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.
